questions.question: Why does SimCLR need batch sizes of 512–8192 while supervised ImageNet training works with batch 256?
options: Each sample needs many negative examples to produce a useful contrastive signal; the batch itself is the pool of negatives. Small batches starve the InfoNCE loss of negatives and training collapses or stalls
SimCLR is slower and needs more GPU memory per step
Batch normalisation statistics require large batches
Contrastive loss divides by batch size so small batches cause gradient explosion
questions.explanation: InfoNCE ranks the positive pair against all other samples in the batch. A batch of 32 gives 30 negatives per positive; 1024 gives 2046. More negatives = sharper contrastive signal. MoCo introduced a momentum queue of past features so effective negatives scale beyond the batch size — this is the standard trick when GPU memory limits batch size.

questions.question: In the InfoNCE loss formula, temperature tau=0.1 is the SimCLR default. What is the effect of increasing tau to 0.5?
options: Training collapses immediately because the loss becomes zero
Each positive pair receives a larger gradient regardless of its current distance

The softmax distribution becomes softer (more uniform), reducing the penalty on negatives and weakening the contrastive signal; lower tau sharpens the distribution and forces more precise embedding alignment
The number of effective negatives increases, improving sample efficiency
questions.explanation: tau divides the cosine similarities before the softmax. Lower tau → sharper distribution → the loss strongly penalises any negative that is not far from the anchor. Higher tau → flatter distribution → the loss treats all negatives similarly and the contrastive signal weakens. The SimCLR paper found tau=0.1 optimal across architectures.

questions.question: In MAE, the small decoder receives the encoder's visible-patch features. What else does it receive at the masked positions?
options: Zero vectors at every masked position
Mask tokens — shared learned embeddings that represent hidden patches; the decoder uses these plus the visible features to reconstruct the original pixels at masked locations
The original unmasked pixel values as a reconstruction shortcut
Random noise sampled from the encoder's feature distribution
questions.explanation: MAE's asymmetric design: the encoder only processes the visible 25% of patches. The decoder receives encoder outputs at visible positions and a single shared learnable mask token at each of the 75% masked positions. The decoder must infer the hidden content from these tokens, forcing the encoder to capture global semantics in its visible-patch representations.

questions.question: The TwoViewDataset.__getitem__ method applies two different random augmentations to the same image and returns them without labels. Why are class labels not used during self-supervised pretraining?
options: The base dataset has no labels available
Labels cannot be loaded from disk efficiently enough for SSL training
Including labels would bias the augmentation policy toward class-discriminative features

Self-supervised learning defines its training signal from the data structure itself — two augmented views of the same image are positives, all other batch items are negatives — so class labels are unnecessary during pretraining and would limit applicability to unlabelled data
questions.explanation: The power of SSL is that it works on completely unlabelled data: web crawls, webcam footage, satellite imagery. The pretext task (matching two views of the same image) provides the signal. Labels are only used later for linear probing or fine-tuning evaluation, not during pretraining itself.

questions.question: MAE masks 75% of patches while BERT masks only 15% of tokens. Why the difference?
options: 75% is an arbitrary hyperparameter that was not carefully ablated
BERT's architecture requires exactly 15% by design

Image patches have low entropy — neighbouring pixels are highly correlated — so masking only 15% would be trivially solvable by local spatial extrapolation. Masking 75% forces the encoder to build semantic representations to reconstruct the hidden patches
Text tokens carry more information per unit than image patches so fewer can be masked
questions.explanation: Information density governs the right mask ratio. Natural language: 15% is sufficient because each masked token has many plausible completions requiring genuine language understanding. Images: unmasked neighbours almost determine a masked patch's pixels, so low mask ratios are solved by interpolation without representation learning. The MAE paper ablates mask ratio and finds performance peaks near 75% for ViT.

questions.question: After self-supervised pretraining, the 'linear probe' evaluation trains only what?
options: The entire encoder with a very low learning rate
The positional embedding layer of the frozen ViT
A full MLP classifier head with 3 hidden layers
A single linear classifier on top of the frozen encoder features; this isolates feature quality from fine-tuning dynamics and is the standard SSL benchmark
questions.explanation: Linear probe freezes all encoder weights and fits Linear(feature_dim → num_classes) on a labelled downstream dataset. Accuracy measures the linear separability of the learned feature space — a direct proxy for feature quality. Fine-tuning the full backbone adds non-linear adaptation capacity and typically gains 2–5 points, but mixes in optimisation effects; both numbers are reported in SSL papers.
